





**Capstone Proposal                                                                                         Russell Bray                    Loyola University Maryland                                                                          1/28/26**

**StatisticalAnalysis Framework for Emotion Recognition Model Evaluation**

**TERMINOLOGY**

·         AffectNet– one of the largest publicly available ‘in-the-wild’ datasets for facialexpression recognition (FER) including over one million images, withapproximately 450,000 manually annotated images depicting human emotions (Mahoor,n.d.).

·         Base model – machine learning (ML) model servingas the starting point for subsequent pre-training.

·         Data Wall – condition in which no morehuman-generated data is publicly available.

·         EfficientNet – convolutional neural network(CNN) architecture used in a family of ML models designed for efficiency; modelEfficientNet-B0 forms the backbone of the base model used in the currentproject (GitHub, EfficientNet, n.d.).

·         EmotiEffLib – evolved version of the HSEmotionlibrary for emotion and engagement recognition in photos and videos that can beused in Python and C++, with backend support for PyTorch and ONNX, enablingefficient real-time analysis across various platforms.

·         EmotiEffNet – family of specialized deepCNNs for facial emotion recognition using  EfficientNet architecture as its foundational backbone (GitHub,EmotiEffNet, n.d.).

·         Emotion AI – sub-disciple of artificial intelligence(AI) focused on simulating, reacting, understanding, and measuring systemswhich involve human emotions.

·         Fine-tuning – customizing an ML model to fulfilla purpose for which it was not originally trained.

·         Foundational model – large, powerful AI modeltrained on massive, broad datasets that learns general patterns, enabling it tobe adapted (fine-tuned) for a wide variety of specific tasks.

·         Gate A (requirements) – in order to be eligiblefor subsequent phases of the project, including deployment, models must surpassthis initial, performance threshold.

·         HSEmotion (High-speed face emotion recognition) –Python library optimized for efficient and lightweight facial expressionrecognition (FER) utilizing EmotiEffNet models (based on EfficientNetarchitecture) as core model types (ScienceDirect, n.d.).

·         Human-Robot-Interaction (HRI) – sub-disciplein robotics focused on interaction between physical robots and human users (InteractionDesign Foundation, n.d.).

·         ImageNet– vast (14+ million), publicly available image dataset designed for visualobject recognition. The dataset continues to play a critical role in theadvancement of deep learning and computer vision (ImageNet, n.d.).

·         Luma AI (Luma Labs) – AI company focused onbuilding multimodal generative AI models that understand and create videocontent (Luma AI, n.d.).

·         OpenCV(Open Source Computer Vision Library) – powerful, open-source computer vision (CV)and machine learning library with 2,500+ algorithms optimized for real-time CV andimage processing.  Initially developed inC/C++, active development includes a Python interface (OpenCV, n.d.).

·         State-of-the-art (models) – ML models performinghighest in industry-standard benchmarks.

·         Synthetic data – data not created by humans.

·         VGGFace2– large-scale (3.3+ million) ‘in-the-wild,’ publicly available dataset designedfor face recognition, specifically aimed at improving recognition acrossvariations in pose, age, and illumination (Cao, n.d.).

**PROJECT BACKGROUND & MOTIVATION**

This proposal presents a rigorous statistical framework forevaluating emotion recognition models in a robotics context, specificallycomparing base versus fine-tuned variants of an HSEmotion model based on afoundational EmotiEffNet model and EfficientNet architecture.  For context, consider the so-called _DataWall_ encountered by engineers while building foundational models. Simplytraining models on more data has become increasingly difficult, and in somecases impossible. Model development increasingly relies on the use of _syntheticdata_.  Companies with billion-dollarvaluations operate intensely to create useful synthetic data because further AIadvancements will depend on vast quantities of such AI-generated data.  Pundits and practitioners debate theeffectiveness and safety concerns associated with training new AI models usingAI-generated data.  This research projectseeks to assess the value of improving a state-of-the-art (SOTA) classificationmodel by the use of synthetic videos for model fine-tuning.

**Research questions**

1) How well does a SOTA base model perform classifying syntheticvideos?

2) Does the base model, fine-tuned on 9,000 synthetic videos/90,000frames, perform better classifying synthetic videos compared to the base model?

3) Does the fine-tuned variant produce statisticallysignificant changes in classification behavior, and if so, which emotionclasses drive the observed differences?

**Statistical approaches**

Univariate statistical methods, applied to each model individually,determine whether a model meets Gate A requirements.  Statistics include the following: Macro F1,Balanced Accuracy, F1(Neutral).

Multivariate statistical methods compare models to determinethe statistical significance of differing performance profiles.  These statistics include the following: Stuart-MaxwellTest (Multi-Class Extension), testing whether the overall distribution ofpredictions shifted between models across all classes simultaneously, and Per-ClassPaired t-Tests with Benjamini-Hochberg Correction, identifying specific emotionclasses show statistically significant improvement or degradation afterfine-tuning, controlling false discovery rate.

**Motivation**

This on-going debate motivates the researcher to evaluateand compare two image classification models - a state-of-the-art (SOTA) basemodel and another version of the same model fine-tuned on synthetic data (videos).  The videos include humans displaying threeemotions:  happy, sad, or neutral. Thedata analysis research adds value to the AI community by quantifying the effectof synthetic data on a widely-accepted SOTA model.  A considerable challenge associated with thiswork involves the time-consuming process of prompting a video-generation model toproduce 10,000 videos. This challenge is worthwhile due to the potentialbroader implications of the findings contributing to the general acceptance andmodels training/fine-tuned on synthetic data.

**SOURCE & NATURE OF DATA**

Data sourcesinclude the following two varieties:  pre-training datasets and fine-tuning synthetic data.

**Pre-trainingdatasets:**

ImageNet (14+ million images of various objects),VGGFace2 (3.31 million faces) and AffectNet (~450K human facial expression),each of which recognized as industry-standards in Emotion AI, form the pre-trainingdatasets of the base model.

**Fine-tuningdataset:**

Forming theprimary focus of the current research, fine-tuning data consists exclusively ofAI-generated, synthetic videos created by the Luma Labs video generation model(Ray 3.14).  OpenCV samples 9,000 videos generatedusing manual prompts, producing a total of 90,000 images (sample 2frames/second or 10 frames/video).  90%of these images form the training dataset with the remaining 10% allocated fortesting both models.

**TOOLS & METHODS**

**ML Classification models**

**Base model:**

Model name: _enet_b0_8_best_vgaf_

·         Status: successfully downloaded

Model backbone:

·         EfficientNet-B0

Number of backbone parameters:

·         5.3M

Backbone dataset: 

·         ImageNet

Model architecture: 

·         EfficientNet

Pre-trained on the following datasets:

·         VGGFace2

·         AffectNet

ML framework: 

·         PyTorch

Emotion recognition system:

·         EmotiEffLib library (ex-HSEmotion)

Output:

·         8 emotion classes with confidence scores

·         3 emotion classes used in the current project(neutral, happy, sad)

**Fine-tuned variant of the base model:**

Base model fine-tuned with synthetic videos utilizing the followingframework:

·         Extract 10 frames/video from 9,000 syntheticvideos -> 90,000 images

·         Fine-tune on 90,000 synthetic images using adaptedmodel weights

·         Validate each epoch on AffectNet validation dataset

·         Select best model based on real validationaccuracy

·         Final evaluation tests selected model usingAffectNet test set  

**Specific hardware and software required to complete theproject:**

Software:

·         TAO Toolkit

o   Low-code AI framework that enables developers tocreate custom, production-ready computer vision and conversational AI modelswithout extensive AI expertise.

o   Uses transfer learning to fine-tune NVIDIApre-trained models with custom data, accelerating development and optimizingfor high-performance inference.

Hardware:

·         NVIDA GPU

o   Model: RTX 4080 Super

o   Architecture: Ada Lovelace

o   Memory: 16GB GDDR6X

**Statistical Approaches & Methods**

Univariate statistics for each model provide actionablenumbers to meet quality gate requirements.  Models must meet these _Gate A_ requirements in order to beeligible for subsequent phases of the project, including deployment.

·         Data analysis of model performance to meet qualitygate requirements:

             ![](data:image/png;base64,)

* **Macro F1** — Primary quality‑gate score summarizing precision/recall balance.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

* **Balanced Accuracy** — Recall‑averaged accuracy to reduce imbalance bias.

![](data:image/png;base64,)

o   **F1(Neutral)** – refers to the F1 scorecomputed for the _neutral class specifically_.

§  Includes the mean of precision and recall forneutral predictions alone.

§  If the neutral class suffers from low precisionand low recall, F1 balances both and ensures neutral serves as a reliableanchor for degree-of-emotion measurements downstream (Phase 2).

**Precision for Neutral:**

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Where:

![](data:image/png;base64,)

**Recall for Neutral:**

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Where:

![](data:image/png;base64,)

**F1 for Neutral:**

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Multivariate statistics compare the performance of these twomodels for the expressed purpose of confirming whether the performance profilesshifted in a statistically significant manner. The analysis should meet theoverall goal of demonstrating benefits realized by the augmentation of  SOTA models with synthetic data.

**Model comparison:  Stuart-MaxwellTest (Multi-Class Extension)**

Purpose: Stuart-Maxwell Test determines whether the overalldistribution of predictions shifted between models across all classessimultaneously.

For K emotion classes, construct a _K×K_ contingencytable where entry _(i,j)_ counts samples with base model predicting class _i_ and fine-tuned model predicting class _j_.

**Marginal differences:**

![](data:image/png;base64,)

where _ni__⋅__​_  is row marginal (base model predicts class _i_)and _n__⋅_ _i_ ​ is column marginal (fine-tuned model predictsclass _i_).

**Covariance matrix of d (excluding one class fornon-singularity):**

![](data:image/png;base64,)

For the reduced vector **_d_** of length _K-1_:

**Test statistic:**

![](data:image/png;base64,)

Under _H₀_ (marginal homogeneity), χ2SM​~ χ²(K-1).

**Diagnostic: Per-class paired t-tests with Benjamini-Hochbergcorrection**

Purpose:  Per-classpaired t-tests with Benjamini-Hochberg correction identifies specific emotionclasses showing statistically significant improvements or degradation afterfine-tuning, thereby controlling false discovery rate.

For each emotion class _c_, compute F1 scores across _K_ folds for both models.

**Paired t-statistic for class _c_:**

![](data:image/png;base64,)

where  ![](data:image/png;base64,) is the mean F1 difference and _sdc_​​is the standard deviation of differences across folds.

**Benjamini-Hochberg procedure:**

![](data:image/png;base64,)

**PROJECT PLAN**

**Milestones**                                                                             

**Timerequirement      Completion date** 

Database                                             2 weeks                       mid February  

Web app                                              1 week                         late February  

Machine learning                                2weeks                       early March    

Workflow automations                       2 weeks                       mid March      

Statistical analysis                               2 weeks                       early April         

Prepare presentation & paper            1 week                         midApril           

**ASSUMPTIONS & POTENTIAL RISKS**

The deployed version of themodel classifies user emotions in real-time. While such predictions often invokeprivacy concerns, the system architecture minimizes these issues by performingall inferences in a closed-loop environment.  The project does not depend on any cloud-based services.  The project supports all required MLoperations (MLOps) locally.

**REFERENCES**

Cao, Q.,Shen, L., Xie, W., Parkhi, O., Zisserman, A. (n.d.). Visual Geometry Group. https://www.robots.ox.ac.uk/~vgg/data/vgg_face2/

OpenCV. (n.d.). _About_. https://opencv.org/about/
